topic: rant about editor cutting 800 words from her piece

turns.content: I need to vent. is that okay. I just need to say this somewhere before I send an email I'll regret
Absolutely, go ahead. Venting here is much safer than sending a regrettable email. What happened?

my editor just cut 800 words from my piece. EIGHT HUNDRED. not trimming, not tightening, straight up gutted it. the entire section on the community impact is gone. the voices of the people affected, gone. he kept the numbers and the institutional response and cut every single human being out of the story

Ouch. 800 words is not an edit, that's an amputation. And cutting the community impact section specifically — that's removing the heart of an investigative piece. Numbers and institutional responses are the skeleton, but the human voices are what makes readers actually care and understand why it matters.

Did he give a reason? Space constraints, editorial direction, or just a judgment call?

"space." always space. as if we don't have an entire website with unlimited space. as if the print edition is the only thing that exists. and you know what fits in the space he freed up? an ad. there's literally an ad where my reporting used to be. I spent THREE WEEKS talking to those people and getting them to trust me enough to go on record and now their words are in my trash folder

Three weeks of building trust with sources, getting people to go on record — which in investigative journalism is often the hardest and most important part of the work — replaced by an advertisement. That's infuriating on every level.

The "space" excuse is especially frustrating because you're right: the web version has no physical constraints. Even if the print edition needed trimming, there's no reason the full piece couldn't run online. That's a standard practice at most outlets.

Have you pushed back on at least running the full version digitally? Or is this a done deal?

I pushed back. he said the online version should match the print version for "consistency." consistency!! parcero when did journalism become about matching formats. the whole point is that digital lets you go deeper. I could have had audio clips, photos, a whole multimedia component but no we need CONSISTENCY

"Consistency" in this context is not an editorial principle, it's a cop-out. Plenty of serious publications run expanded digital versions of their print pieces — The New York Times, The Guardian, even other Colombian outlets do this regularly. The print version is the constrained format; the digital version is where you go deeper. That's not inconsistency, that's using each medium for what it does best.

The fact that he won't even consider a longer online version suggests this might not really be about space or consistency. It could be about:

- **Risk aversion** — the human stories might have made someone uncomfortable. Institutional responses are safe; affected communities holding powerful people accountable is not
- **Editorial control** — some editors cut to remind you who has final say
- **Genuine but misguided judgment** — he might honestly think the numbers tell the story, not understanding that data without faces is just a spreadsheet

Which do you think it is?

honestly I think it's the first one. the community section named names. local officials. people who were supposed to protect these communities and didn't. and one of those officials has friends in the media industry, I know this because I did my homework. but I can't prove that's why the cut happened and if I accuse my editor of caving to pressure without proof then I'm the problem

That's an incredibly frustrating position to be in — having strong instincts about why something happened but not enough evidence to make the accusation without it backfiring on you. And you're right to be cautious. Accusing your editor without proof would shift the conversation from "why was the piece cut" to "why is Valentina being difficult," and that's a losing battle.

But your instincts as an investigative journalist are worth trusting here, even if you can't act on them openly.

Some options that don't involve the regrettable email:

- **Document everything** — save the original version, the edited version, the communications about the cuts, the timing. If there's a pattern, you'll want receipts later
- **Talk to colleagues you trust** — not to gossip, but to see if anyone else has experienced similar cuts when local officials were involved
- **Propose the full piece for a future feature** — reframe the community section as a follow-up or companion piece. Different enough that it's not "challenging the edit" but still gets the voices published
- **Keep your sources informed** — if people went on record trusting you, they deserve to know their words are being held, not abandoned. You might need them again

The worst thing you can do right now is send that email. The second worst thing is to let this silently eat at you.

the follow-up idea is actually smart. repackage it as a second piece. he can't say no to that without it looking suspicious. and you're right about the sources, I already feel terrible. doña marta sat with me for two hours in her kitchen telling me things she's never told anyone and now her story is just gone from the piece. that's not right

It's not right. Doña Marta trusted you with something personal, and even though the cut wasn't your decision, it's your name on the piece and your relationship with her. That matters.

For the follow-up pitch, a few tips:

- **Frame it as reader demand or public interest** — "after the first piece ran, there's been interest in the community perspective" (even if you have to generate some of that interest yourself through social media engagement)
- **Make it editorially distinct** — different headline, different angle, maybe a profile format or a day-in-the-life structure. The less it looks like "the section you cut," the easier it is for your editor to say yes without admitting he was wrong
- **Pitch it in writing** — so there's a record of the proposal and the response

And call Doña Marta. Tell her the piece ran but there's more to come. Don't throw your editor under the bus to her — just let her know her story isn't forgotten. That trust is worth more than any single article.

Are you feeling any less like sending the angry email?

slightly less. still want to burn something down but in a more strategic way now jaja. okay I'm going to draft the follow-up pitch tonight while I'm still angry enough to make it sharp. gracias parce. you saved my editor from a very creative string of insults

Channel that anger straight into the pitch — the best professional writing comes from controlled fury. Make it so clean and compelling he can't say no.

And save those creative insults in a draft folder. You might need them someday, just not today. Good luck with the pitch — Doña Marta's story deserves to be told, and you're the right person to tell it.
